---
title: "How I Stopped Copy-Pasting Numbers Into My Thesis"
author: "Kyle Cahitas"
date: 2026-05-20
categories: [reproducibility, R, thesis]
description: "Every statistic in my thesis lives in one file. Nothing gets typed by hand. Here is why."
---

Early on I made a mistake that a lot of people make. I ran an analysis, saw a number, and typed it into my draft. Then I re-ran the analysis two weeks later, the number changed slightly, and I updated the draft in three places. I was pretty sure I got all three. I was not certain.

That uncertainty is the whole problem. Once you hand-copy a number, the draft and the code can drift apart, and you have no way to prove they still agree.

So now every reported statistic in my thesis gets pulled out of the analysis script exactly once, into a single file. Call it a numbers ledger. The paper, the poster, an email to my advisor, they all point at that one file and nothing else. No number gets typed by hand anywhere downstream.

When I re-run a model and a value shifts, it shifts in one place. Everything that depends on it follows. I never go hunting through the draft wondering if I caught every mention.

It is a small habit and it is a little tedious to set up. It also removed a whole category of quiet error, the kind that used to keep me up before a deadline. Worth it.
